title: DylanOFM — Complete Playbook (18 videos)
summary: Across 18 videos DylanOFM teaches one core thesis: an OFM agency scales by becoming a productized talent agency — one tight creator avatar, one standardized fulfillment system, one acquisition engine — where the creator is the only variable, rather than doing bespoke per-creator work or betting the business on one or two big models. The single biggest takeaway for Palm is to treat agency growth as a repeatable manufacturing process (avatar-first, data-driven content, brand-led inbound acquisition, deliberate deal structure) and to instrument creator LTV/retention so acquisition and pricing decisions are made on data, not feel.
analyst_note: Numbers are almost entirely self-reported and unverifiable. Dylan claims a $1M-$2M/month agency (he cites both $1.6M and ~$2M), '$20M generated,' 'helped 12 / over 550 agencies,' and case studies like Peachy 12k->282k in 60 days — yet his YouTube channel has only ~8,410 subscribers and most videos pull 600-1,900 views; his one breakout (~46k views) is the $364k Cape Town lifestyle flex, not proof of agency results. No third-party verification, no audited figures, and the content is overtly a funnel into paid workshops/coaching ($97 VIP seats, $5k setup fees, $50k coaching offer, repeated 'OFM scaling roadmap' lead magnet). Treat every dollar/result claim as marketing. He also leans on a misquoted 'US Bureau of Labor Statistics' stat repeatedly. HOWEVER, the underlying frameworks are coherent, internally consistent across 10+ videos, and align with legitimate direct-response and ops principles (avatar discipline, LTV-over-CPL, productization, brand-as-distribution, platform-risk hygiene). The advice is genuinely useful as build-toward R&D and as a structured lens for Palm's documented gaps, independent of whether his revenue claims are real.

findings.title: Productize the agency: one avatar, one offer, one fulfillment system — the creator is the only variable
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's central, most-repeated thesis is that scaling models does not scale money; productizing the agency does. Every creator gets the SAME standardized delivery — same onboarding, content verticals, posting cadence, chat system, funnel, welcome message — and the creator herself is treated as the only variable. He claims identical-process creators diverge wildly (e.g. Peachy 12k->282k/mo in 60 days vs another 10k->30k) on the same system with no extra effort, and that after productizing, 50 models became more profitable and easier than 5. He explicitly evolved from his old tiered 'ABC system' to a single productized baseline.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a genuinely productized SOCIAL-content engine — the 8-step inspo-driven pipeline that picks what to film from viral-score data (§2). But Palm has no documented productized OPERATING system spanning offer, onboarding, pricing, and acquisition; onboarding is a mechanical form wizard (§1) and there's no standard offer or pricing (§6). Palm also has a lever Dylan lacks: the AI-persona stream (§0) removes the 'capped by real creators you can sign' ceiling, making a productized fulfillment spec even higher-leverage for Palm.
findings.recommendation: Write down Palm's creator avatar(s) and a single standardized fulfillment spec (content archetypes, cadence, chat handoff, funnel, pricing) so any new real OR AI creator drops into a repeatable pipeline instead of bespoke setup. Extend the inspo pipeline's productization discipline to offer, onboarding, and the eventual in-house/AI chat layer.

sources.quote: what actually scales is having one avatar, one way of fulfilling for that avatar, one offer
the biggest variable in the productiz fulfillment system... is the creator
we did nothing different from this girl than we did for this girl because our agency is productized

findings.title: Avatar discipline + pain-based messaging: 'messaging controls targeting'
findings.what_they_do: Dylan says the #1 leverage point in any agency is a tight creator avatar, and the biggest audit change he makes for $1M/mo agencies is just narrowing the avatar (analyze existing creators for who was easiest to fulfill, highest LTV, fastest results; cut the rest). On top of the avatar he layers 'pain-based messaging' — because you can't target interests on ads/IG, the WORDS you use ARE the targeting. 'Pain equals intent': describe a creator's current/recurring/future/past pain so precisely she feels you're 'reading her diary,' which repels time-wasters and attracts creators already in pain who sign in 24-48h without haggling on the 50%. He says he mapped 100+ customer archetypes within his avatar.
findings.vs_us: Palm onboards anyone who completes the wizard (§1) with no documented avatar or qualification filter, and acquisition is manual DM-led by Josh — the baseline explicitly flags 'no repeatable acquisition machine' as a GAP. Dylan's avatar + pain-messaging is exactly that machine, and maps mainly to Palm's real-creator side (for AI personas, 'acquisition' is account creation, not signing).
findings.recommendation: Define an explicit real-creator avatar from Palm's own best existing creators (easiest to manage, highest revenue, longest tenure) and add a qualification gate before onboarding. Build acquisition messaging around that avatar's specific pains rather than generic 'we'll grow you' language. Capture the avatar definition as a living doc the SMM hub can reference.

sources.quote: Messaging controls targeting
Pain equals intent
the change I make is just narrowing down an avatar and cutting all the [...]

findings.title: IG flagging is structural, not bad luck: the 5-pillar account-survival framework
findings.what_they_do: His most recent video argues Meta's AI (under legal pressure from 41 state AGs + KOSA) hunts romance-scammer / CSAM patterns, and OnlyFans creators look identical to that fingerprint (sexy photo + external link + 'DM me'/'subscribe'). He gives a 5-pillar survival system: (1) content must work as lifestyle content with sex appeal as a secondary hook, context must fit (bikini at beach = fine, bikini at kitchen counter = solicitation); (2) captions/emojis — never name the platform or use solicitation language or sexualized emoji combos, describe the offer without naming it; (3) clean bio/profile/story-highlights (highlights are live-scanned, audit them); (4) a JavaScript-redirect landing page on an owned domain so Meta's crawler (which doesn't execute JS) can't see the OF destination; (5) operational hygiene — max two accounts per creator, one person/one device per account, no multi-login VAs/VPNs (he lost his own 50k-follower account to six logins). He also reports unbans are now near-impossible/expensive after Meta shut down the cheap Indian unban pipeline.
findings.vs_us: This is directly comparable to Palm's strongest defensible asset — the hardware-isolated 90-day AI-account warmup (§4: GrapheneOS, one SIM/IP/email per persona, no early OF link, shadowban monitoring). Palm's warmup already embodies Dylan's pillar 5 (isolation) and pillar 1 (no direct OF link early). Dylan adds NEW, current detail Palm should fold in: the romance-scammer pattern-match framing, the JS-redirect link cloak, story-highlight scanning, audio-fingerprint matching on reposts, and the four-tier ban-response playbook. Highly relevant to BOTH real creators' IG and the AI personas.
findings.recommendation: Fold Dylan's 5 pillars into Palm's account-hygiene SOP for real creators and AI personas: adopt the JS-redirect owned-domain link cloak, audit story highlights, enforce reskin-not-repeat on trial reels (audio fingerprint), keep strict one-person/one-device login, and codify the 4-tier ban-response (don't appeal single-content strikes; self-verify on suspension; never use cheap unban services). His free 29-page SOP is worth pulling for the team.

sources.quote: we look identical to the people that Meta's AI is trying to catch
if you have a JavaScript-based redirect, the crawler reads the page, sees no adult domain... and just moves on
have one phone per account, per creator

findings.title: Data-driven content via the 70/20/10 reskin system — stop inventing new ideas
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's 'viral content system': a viral video is a data signal to replicate, not a one-off. Allocate 70% of content to reskinning proven winners, 20% to tweaks on outliers (3-5x MEDIAN views), 10% to brand-new ideas. Viral = 10x+ median. For creators with no proven content, run the 'foundational vertical cycle' — pick 3 proven verticals, collect 30 viral examples each (90 total), remake all 90 over 30 days to generate data, then switch to 70/20/10. Require 3-6 posts/day (he says 6x/day IG + up to 20 trial reels/day). He names evergreen verticals that still work in 2026: Omegle, Snapchat, skits, talking-head, day-in-the-life, plus AI carousels (Nano Banana Pro) now converting better than reels on some accounts.
findings.vs_us: Closest analog to Palm's strength. Palm's inspo pipeline (§2) already scrapes inspiration reels, AI-scores virality, and promotes ideas to creator boards. Dylan adds: an explicit 70/20/10 budget, reskinning the creator's OWN winners (not just external inspo), median-based outlier math, named evergreen verticals, and the carousel insight. Palm's strategy engine (§3, in-flight) rotates pillars but isn't documented as enforcing a reskin ratio.
findings.recommendation: Add a 70/20/10 reskin allocation + median-based outlier detection to Palm's strategy engine, feed each creator's own viral/outlier posts back in as reskin sources, and test the named verticals + daily AI carousels. Low-cost upgrades to an asset Palm already has.

sources.quote: the 70 2010 rule... 70% reskinning the proven winners, 20% small tweaks on outliers, 10% brand new ideas
anything that does 3 to 5x your median is now classed as an outlier
AI carousels on Instagram also work extremely well... the conversion... is better than Reels on a lot of accounts

findings.title: Optimize for ROI/LTV, not cost-per-lead — and instrument creator lifetime value
findings.what_they_do: Dylan repeatedly attacks cost-per-lead as a vanity metric: the only acquisition metric that matters is ROI tied to creator LTV (a $500 CPL with $5k LTV beats a $2 CPL with near-zero LTV). He insists you must track average creator retention ('is it 6 months or 3 months?') and average revenue per creator, then build 1% lookalikes off ~100 avatar-matched leads. He also frames 'do the math' backwards: retainer x retention = LTV per client, then meetings/close-rate/outreach needed to hit a revenue goal. His 300k-plateau video adds: rank daily tasks by revenue > retention > leverage and delete the rest; act at 30-50% certainty; sunk cost is dead.
findings.vs_us: Palm tracks per-creator commission and weekly invoices (§6) but has no documented creator-LTV, retention-duration, or CAC instrumentation; retention is a flagged GAP (§7). Without average tenure and revenue-per-creator, Palm cannot rationally set an acquisition budget, evaluate paid channels, or price. This is foundational financial-modeling hygiene Dylan treats as table stakes.
findings.recommendation: Instrument two numbers Palm can likely derive from existing Airtable data — average creator tenure (months) and average lifetime revenue per creator, split real vs AI. These unlock rational acquisition-spend, pricing, and avatar-selection decisions and directly start filling the §7 retention gap with measurement.

sources.quote: cost per lead is a bad metric... the focus metric always is ROI
what's the average time that they stay with us? Is it 6 months? Is it 3 months?
rule number three is what I call the math

findings.title: Deal structure and price are the biggest untapped lever — move beyond 50/50 rev-share
findings.what_they_do: Dylan calls deal structure 'the biggest lever in almost any business' and one of the three biggest agency levers (with acquisition + productization). He charges a $5k upfront setup fee PLUS 50% back-end, says it acts as a filter for serious creators and gives the agency leverage/cash on day one, and claims an 18-model month at a 50% close rate with it. He argues 50/50 rev-share is not a law of physics: use performance hybrids, upfront+rev-share, tiered/milestone splits, and an ascension model (productized baseline -> high-touch custom tier with clipping/paid traffic for proven creators). He cites raising his coaching price to $50k and immediately signing six $500k+/mo agencies as proof price filters for better clients.
findings.vs_us: PURE GAP for Palm. Commission % is set per-creator at onboarding and tracked (§6), but there is NO documented pricing model, standard split, fee structure, tiered pricing, or rationale — and commission is the #1 creator concern in Palm's own market research. Dylan's deal-structure thinking is exactly the 'no internal baseline yet' area (§6) the agent should surface for Palm to fill.
findings.recommendation: Treat this as build-toward intel for the §6 gap: explore deal structures beyond a single split (e.g. setup fee + rev-share, tiered by support level, ascension tier for proven creators) and decide a documented standard. Even if Palm keeps rev-share, write down the rationale and a tiering plan. An upfront fee also acts as a creator-quality filter (ties to the avatar discipline in finding #2).

sources.quote: deal structure... is the biggest lever in almost any business
We even charge 5K up front as a setup fee... 50/50 on the back end... 50% close rate. We closed 18 models last month
some of you are treating like these 50/50 rev share... like this law of physics in the industry. It is not

findings.title: Brand-led inbound is the real moat: 'your constraint is irrelevance, not leads'
findings.what_they_do: Dylan argues the real growth constraint is irrelevance ('nobody knows you exist'), not leads. He pushes a founder/agency brand as the highest-leverage acquisition channel: post ~6x/day, document outcomes, let creators come to you as the authority. Three laws (volume negates luck, clarity beats cleverness, proof conquers disbelief) and four influence levers (status, power, credibility, likeness). He says inbound flips every conversation from zero trust to a 'surplus of trust' and eventually ~80% of pipeline is inbound. In the $500k-plays video he names YouTube long-form as his highest-LTV, lowest-cost lead source and adds lump-sum events (weekly cold webinars, quarterly 3-day challenges) as current high-performers.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is manual DM-led by Josh + referrals (§1), the flagged 'no repeatable acquisition machine' GAP. Brand-led inbound is that machine; maps to the real-creator side. Pairs with finding #2 (avatar/messaging) — the brand content IS where pain-based messaging gets distributed.
findings.recommendation: Stand up a deliberate founder/agency-brand presence (outcome-documenting, low-friction) as a real-creator acquisition channel to reduce dependence on Josh's manual DMs; YouTube/long-form is the highest-LTV channel per Dylan. Start the cheap part now: install a proof-capture mechanism routing every creator win/testimonial to a table.

sources.quote: your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
volume negates luck
YouTube continues to be one of the highest converting free things that you can do

findings.title: Concentration risk: never let one or two creators carry the business
findings.what_they_do: Dylan repeatedly warns about over-exposure to one model — he watched agencies doing $200k/mo on two models 'go back to zero overnight,' and it happened to him (his big model left, he went back to zero). He frames single-creator dependence as gambling and as the emotional case for productization + continuous acquisition: the only protection is a repeatable system signing avatar-fit creators continuously so no one departure is existential. He explicitly rejects the 'just vertically scale 3 creators to $1M' path as a 'special snowflake' bet with high risk and zero longevity.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a built-in structural hedge competitors lack: the AI-persona stream (§0) diversifies revenue away from any single real creator and can't 'leave.' That meaningfully reduces the concentration risk Dylan obsesses over. But on the real-creator side Palm has no documented retention/churn-risk monitoring (§7 gap), so a high-earning real creator leaving would currently be unmanaged.
findings.recommendation: Track revenue concentration (share of total from top 1-2 real creators) as a risk KPI; lean on the AI-persona stream as deliberate diversification. Pair with the retention instrumentation from finding #5 so a key creator's disengagement is detected before churn.

sources.quote: I have literally watched agencies doing 200K a month with two models go back to zero overnight
If one model... could cut your AY's income in half or worse, then your agency is not a real business

findings.title: Theory of One + amplify what works: master one channel, then pour volume in
findings.what_they_do: For $15k-$100k agencies Dylan prescribes the 'theory of one': fix fulfillment first (one predictable traffic source, one chat system), then ONE acquisition channel (outreach, inbound, referrals, or paid — pick one), then scale by pushing more volume through the same system. He says the #1 plateau mistake is adding Reddit/Twitter/threads/paid simultaneously and spreading thin ('simplicity scales, complexity fails'). His matched complaint at 100-300k is the failure to AMPLIFY: people find something that works (e.g. a 3x-ROAS ad or a viral content system) and keep it small or chase a new shiny thing instead of scaling it until it faces entropy. 'All growth is a proven loop + more reps + fix the constraint + repeat.'
findings.vs_us: Palm's content side already follows a focused model (inspo pipeline + IG/TikTok), consistent with 'one traffic source.' The risk this surfaces: as Palm systematizes SMM (§10) across dual real+AI streams, it could over-fragment. Traffic acquisition is a flagged GAP (§8) — when Palm builds a traffic playbook, 'master one, then amplify' is the sequencing rule.
findings.recommendation: When Palm formalizes a traffic/acquisition playbook (§8 gap), prove one channel to predictability before adding others, then deliberately scale volume through winners rather than diversifying prematurely. Apply the same to AI personas — nail one platform's warmup-to-monetization loop before fanning out.

sources.quote: you need to do it by the theory of one... you just need one traffic source
You find something that works and then you keep it small
when you find something that works, you have to go all in

findings.title: The lead-magnet -> VSL -> call funnel, built backwards from one offer angle
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's signing funnel: a high-value free lead magnet (must solve ~25% of the main offer for free, e.g. a 'chat money map' PDF + Loom), a 5-8 min VSL whose only two jobs are (1) get qualified creators to book and (2) pre-handle objections, then a call, then a 1-2 min pre-call VSL to raise show rate. Built backwards — lead magnet first, then VSL, then ads last — so messaging stays coherent hook-to-call. The offer picks ONE angle (speed, risk-reduction, or ease) plus a concrete numeric guarantee ('2,000 paying subs in 30 days or we work for free'). Claims it pre-closes 30-40% of booked calls.
findings.vs_us: Palm's marketing-site funnel is a relationship pitch (Talk -> Custom Strategy -> Onboarding -> Weekly Check-ins, §1), not a direct-response qualify-and-close funnel with a lead magnet, VSL, or numeric guarantee, and Palm has no standard offer/guarantee (§6 gap). Build-toward intel for making the real-creator signing motion repeatable and self-qualifying.
findings.recommendation: Pilot a single-angle offer with one concrete guarantee and a lead magnet that gives away real value (Palm's inspo/viral-content know-how is a natural lead magnet), feeding a short qualifying VSL before a call. This also forces Palm to finally write down a standard offer (part of §6).

sources.quote: a funnel that books qualified creator calls on autopilot and pre-closes about 30 to 40% of them
the lead magnet must solve a quarter of your main offer for free
choose one main angle... speed, risk, and ease

findings.title: Replace yourself / build a business not a job; A-players over donkeys
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's scaling end-state is decentralizing the founder so the business runs without you (he claims he took 3 months off and revenue rose). He prescribes a simplify -> eliminate -> automate -> delegate loop, a 'value actions' focus on only four things (generate appointments, close, deliver results, systemize/automate), and an 'evolution phases' model: do it yourself to prove a process, build SOPs/a 'war chest,' then hire and teach it. He's blunt that agencies must hire A-players and stop tolerating cheap 'donkeys' — paying $2k more for a better hire, or a $20k/mo A-player, is what broke him past the 300k plateau. Brand is framed as the founder's AI-proof moat.
findings.vs_us: Palm's team is small and role-defined in software (super_admin, admin, editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) but the baseline flags a GAP (§9): no org chart, hiring criteria, role scorecards, or scaling plan. This is build-toward intel for Palm's growth, especially as it brings chatting in-house (§5 roadmap) and grows the AI-persona operation — both need defined roles and a hiring bar.
findings.recommendation: As Palm scales (in-house chat team, more AI personas), document role scorecards and a hiring bar (the §9 gap) so growth doesn't depend on the founders being in every loop, and budget for A-player hires rather than cheap labor at the points that gate growth. Treat the agency brand as a founder-owned strategic asset.

sources.quote: build a business not a job
the people who hire better will always make more money... stop tolerating the absolute [...] donkeys
hiring my first A player for like 20 odd grand a month salary was what actually broke me past that revenue range

run_notes: Coverage: read the Palm baseline, both meta JSONs, and effectively all 18 transcripts (full verbatim on ~16; the cold-outreach 30-models video o4s_nKjm2fw and replace-yourself fMV9M9Ldylo were covered via their thematic overlap with the funnel/brand/100k/300k videos rather than read end-to-end, so cold-outreach mechanics and the literal replace-yourself playbook are the thinnest spots — worth a targeted re-read next run). PROCESS NOTE: the tool-output channel intermittently failed mid-run; some Read/Bash calls returned blank or 'wasted call,' so a few duplicate reads occurred, but every citation above comes from a transcript actually read, and timestamps are taken from the &t=NNNs nearest each claim. THEMES THAT REPEATED across nearly every video: avatar discipline (literally every video), volume-negates-luck, reskinning over novelty (70/20/10), productization + 'the creator is the variable,' brand/inbound as the real moat, LTV-over-CPL, deal-structure/price as a top lever, and 'simplicity scales, complexity fails.' CONTRADICTS PALM: Dylan would push real-creator brand-led inbound, an upfront-fee deal structure, and a single standardized offer/guarantee, whereas Palm runs manual DM-led signing with no standard offer or pricing — but Palm's AI-persona lever, inspo-data content engine, and hardware-isolated warmup are genuine advantages he doesn't have. HIGHEST-VALUE NEW INTEL beyond the obvious: the IG-flagging 5-pillar framework (finding #3) is timely and directly upgrades Palm's §4 account-hygiene SOP for BOTH real and AI accounts; deal-structure (finding #6) and LTV instrumentation (finding #5) hit Palm's flagged pricing/retention gaps hardest. WATCH NEXT: pull Dylan's free 29-page IG-survival SOP; consider a focused digest on the chatting/PPV creators in the library (habibi, Luca Pritchard, Henry Hien) as build-toward intel for Palm's in-house + AI chat roadmap (§5).
